projects.title: Unit 8 Project — Statistics in Action
projects.desc: Capstone: pose a statistical question, collect data, and tell the story behind the numbers.

projects.title: Statistics of My Life
projects.desc: Exemplar project — turn real questions about your own life into a data investigation.

projects.title: Statistical Questions Data Lab
projects.desc: Flagship lab on recognizing statistical questions and the variability in data (6.SP.1).

projects.title: Statistical Questions Game
projects.desc: Sort and identify statistical vs. non-statistical questions in a fast-paced game.

title: Statistical Questions and Data
contentObjective: I can tell the difference between a statistical question and a non-statistical question.
languageObjective: I can explain my reasoning using the words statistical question, data, variability, and survey.

mission.title: Scout the Hardwood
mission.story: You are the new data analyst for the Riverside Hoops youth league. The head coach drops a stack of questions on your desk — some will yield rich data full of variety, others just one fixed fact. Master statistical questions and you decide what the front office actually investigates.
mission.objective: Tell statistical questions from non-statistical ones so the team collects data worth analyzing.

scenes.name: Scouting Report
scenes.text: Review the coach's questions and spot which ones expect varied answers.

scenes.name: Analyst Glossary
scenes.text: Learn the terms: statistical question, data, variability, survey, distribution.

scenes.name: Question Sort
scenes.text: Sort each question into Statistical or Not Statistical to build the scouting plan.

scenes.name: Data Room
scenes.text: Classify the questions. The front office adapts the difficulty to keep you sharp.

scenes.name: Press Box
scenes.text: Help the school paper choose the question that yields the best story data.

scenes.name: Final Report
scenes.text: Submit your analysis and prove you know a statistical question when you see one.

finale.title: Report Filed!
finale.text: Your scouting plan captured exactly the data the coaches needed, and the team is climbing the standings. Riverside Hoops just made you lead analyst — because you know which questions reveal real variability.

vocabulary.definition: A question where the answers will be different for different people.
vocabulary.definitionEs: Una pregunta cuyas respuestas serán distintas para distintas personas.
vocabulary.visual: "How tall are the players on the team?" → answers vary: 60 in, 63 in, 58 in, 65 in
vocabulary.cloze: A question that expects a variety of answers and is answered by collecting data is a ___.

examples.text: How tall are the students in my class?
examples.why: The answers vary, so data must be collected.
It has one fixed answer, so it is not statistical.

vocabulary.definition: Facts and numbers you collect, like answers to a survey.
vocabulary.definitionEs: Datos y números que recoges, como respuestas a una encuesta.
vocabulary.visual: Player heights: 60 in, 63 in, 58 in, 65 in — four different measurements
vocabulary.cloze: Facts or numbers collected to answer a question are ___.

vocabulary.definition: How spread out the numbers are.
vocabulary.definitionEs: Qué tan separados están los números.
vocabulary.visual: Scores 10, 12, 11 are close together (low variability); scores 2, 15, 30 are far apart (high variability)
vocabulary.cloze: How much the values in a data set differ from one another is the ___.

vocabulary.definition: Collecting facts by asking people questions.
vocabulary.definitionEs: Recoger datos haciendo preguntas a las personas.
vocabulary.visual: Asking 50 students 'What is your favorite sport?' and recording the answers
vocabulary.cloze: A way to collect data by asking many people the same question is a ___.

vocabulary.definition: How the data looks: where it sits and how spread out it is.
vocabulary.definitionEs: Cómo se ven los datos: dónde están y qué tan separados están.
vocabulary.visual: If most scores cluster in the middle with fewer at the edges, the distribution is symmetric
vocabulary.cloze: The way data values are spread out across a range is the ___.

launch.narrative: You've just been hired as the data analyst for a youth basketball league. The head coach hands you a list of questions the staff wants to investigate about their players. Some questions will give you interesting data with variety, while others have just one fixed answer. Your job: figure out which questions are worth investigating with data.
launch.contextImage: Clipboard with player roster, basketball court in background
noticePrompts: Which questions could have many different answers?
Which questions have only one exact answer?
What makes a question good for collecting data?
wonderPrompts: Why would a coach care about questions with variability?
Could a question be reworded to become statistical?

conceptIntro.heading: What makes a question a statistical question?
conceptIntro.intro: A statistical question is one you answer by collecting data, and the answers are different for different people or cases. A non-statistical question has just one fixed answer.
conceptIntro.keyIdea: If you expect the answers to vary, it is a statistical question.

iDo.title: Watch me
lines: Question 1: "How tall is our point guard?" I think about the answer. There is only one point guard, so there is one fixed height, like 70 inches.
One answer, no variety, so this is NOT a statistical question.
Question 2: "How tall is each player on the roster?" The players have different heights: 60 in, 63 in, 58 in, 65 in.
The answers vary from player to player, so I must collect data. This IS a statistical question.

weDo.title: Let's try together
lines: New question: "How many pets does each student in our class have?" Will every student give the same answer, or different answers?
Since some students have 0 pets and others have 2 or 3, the answers vary. So is this statistical or not statistical?
Now a different question: "How many days are in one week?" Does this have one fixed answer or many?

youDo.title: Now it's your turn
lines: Next, you will sort the coach's questions into Statistical or Not Statistical, just like I did.
For each one, ask yourself: will the answers be different, or always the same?

explore.label: Sort each question into the correct category: Statistical Question or Not a Statistical Question.

cards.text: How many points does each player on the team score per game?
How many players are on the roster?
What is the free-throw percentage of each player?
What color are the team jerseys?
How many minutes does each player practice per week?
How many quarters are in a basketball game?
How far can each player throw the ball?
What is the height of each player on the team?

discourse.prompt: What pattern do you notice about statistical questions compared to non-statistical questions?
discourse.sentenceFrame: Statistical questions expect ___ in the answers because ___. Non-statistical questions have only ___ answer because ___.

approaching.stem: Which of the following is a statistical question?
choices: How many points did each player score this season?
How many points are scored for a free throw?
What sport does this team play?
How many halves are in a soccer game?
approaching.explanation: "How many points did each player score?" is statistical because different players will have different scores — there is variability in the data.

approaching.stem: Which question is NOT a statistical question?
choices: How many innings are in a baseball game?
How many home runs did each player hit this year?
How many miles does each runner train per week?
How tall is each player on the basketball team?
approaching.explanation: "How many innings are in a baseball game?" has one fixed answer (9) — no variability. The other questions all produce different answers for different people.

approaching.stem: Why is 'How many hours of sleep did each student get last night?' a statistical question?
choices: Because different students got different amounts of sleep
Because it asks about sleep
Because hours are numbers
Because the answer is always 8 hours
approaching.explanation: A statistical question anticipates variability — different students slept different amounts, so the data will vary.

approaching.stem: Match each question to its type.

pairs.left: How tall is each swimmer on the team?
What color is the team mascot?
How many laps does each runner complete?
How many players are on a soccer team?

approaching.instructions: Sort these questions into Statistical or Not Statistical.

categories.label: Statistical Question
items: How many books did each student read this month?
What grade did each student earn on the test?

categories.label: Not a Statistical Question
items: How many days are in a week?

hints: Statistical questions expect different answers from different people or observations.
Non-statistical questions have one fixed answer that doesn't change.
approaching.label: Sort each question by type.

approaching.title: approaching — Spot the Common Mistake

workedExample.label: Read
workedExample.work: A student is working on Statistical Questions and Data.

workedExample.label: Attempt
workedExample.work: They ignored the rule: If you expect the answers to vary, it is a statistical question.

workedExample.label: Check
workedExample.work: The answer does not match the math rule from class.

approaching.correctWork: Follow the key idea: If you expect the answers to vary, it is a statistical question.
hints: Which step breaks the rule you learned today?
Remember: If you expect the answers to vary, it is a statistical question.

onLevel.stem: A coach asks: "How many hours does each athlete on the track team sleep the night before a meet?" Why is this a statistical question?
choices: Because the answers will vary from athlete to athlete
Because it is about sports
Because it has a numerical answer
Because the coach asked it
onLevel.explanation: A statistical question anticipates variability. Different athletes sleep different amounts, so the data collected will vary.

rows.question: How many push-ups can each student do in one minute?
rows.why: Different students can do different amounts — there is variability.

rows.question: How many sides does a basketball court have?
rows.why: There is one fixed answer (4 sides) — no variability.

rows.question: How far does each player kick the ball?
rows.why: Different players kick different distances — the data varies.

onLevel.label: Decide if each question is statistical and explain why.

onLevel.stem: Match each question with what makes it statistical or not.

pairs.left: How fast does each sprinter run the 100m?
How long is an Olympic pool?
How many goals did each player score this season?
How many innings are in a baseball game?

onLevel.instructions: Each non-statistical question below has been rewritten to become statistical. Match the original to its rewritten version.

categories.label: Original: 'How many students are in 6th grade?'
items: Rewritten: 'How many hours does each 6th grader study per week?'

categories.label: Original: 'What time does school start?'
items: Rewritten: 'What time does each student arrive at school?'

hints: To make a question statistical, add variability by asking about 'each' person or observation.
The rewritten version should produce different answers for different people.
onLevel.label: Match each non-statistical question with its statistical rewrite.

onLevel.title: onLevel — Spot the Common Mistake

workedExample.label: Read
workedExample.work: A student is working on Statistical Questions and Data.

workedExample.label: Attempt
workedExample.work: They ignored the rule: If you expect the answers to vary, it is a statistical question.

workedExample.label: Check
workedExample.work: The answer does not match the math rule from class.

onLevel.correctWork: Follow the key idea: If you expect the answers to vary, it is a statistical question.
hints: Which step breaks the rule you learned today?
Remember: If you expect the answers to vary, it is a statistical question.

extending.title: Find the Reasoning Error

workedExample.label: Question
workedExample.work: How many touchdowns did the quarterback throw this season?

workedExample.label: Student says
workedExample.work: This is NOT a statistical question.

workedExample.label: Reason
workedExample.work: It only asks about one player, so there is no variability.

extending.correctWork: Even though it asks about one player, "this season" means multiple games. The number of touchdowns per game varies, so there IS variability in the data. This IS a statistical question.
hints: Think about whether the data collected would have different values across games.
The question asks about performance over a whole season — each game can have a different result.

extending.prompt: Write one statistical question and one non-statistical question about your school's cafeteria. Explain why each question is or is not statistical, and describe what the data would look like for the statistical question.
extending.sentenceFrame: Statistical question: ___. This is statistical because ___. The data might look like: ___. Non-statistical question: ___. This is not statistical because ___.

rows.nonStatistical: How many players are on the team?
rows.rewritten: How many minutes does each player on the team play per game?
rows.variability: Different players get different amounts of playing time.

rows.nonStatistical: What sport does this league play?
rows.rewritten: How many games does each team in this league win per season?
rows.variability: Different teams win different numbers of games.

extending.label: Rewrite each non-statistical question to make it statistical and explain the variability.

optionalActivity.name: Sports Data Investigation Launch
optionalActivity.intro: The front office hands you a fresh stack of scouting questions — decide which ones anticipate variability and are worth investigating with data.

optional.instructions: Sort each scouting question into Statistical or Not Statistical. A statistical question anticipates that the answers will vary.

items.text: How many steals does each player record per game?
How old is the head coach?
How many ounces of water does each player drink during practice?
What city does the team play its home games in?
How many hours of sleep did each player get before the game?
How many points is a successful field goal worth?
How fast does each player run the length of the court?
What number is on the team captain's jersey?

optional.stem: The front office wants ONE more question that will give varied data to analyze. Which question is statistical?
choices: How many rebounds does each player grab per game?
How many players start the game on the court?
What is the name of the team's arena?
How long is one quarter of the game in minutes?
optional.explanation: "How many rebounds does each player grab per game?" is statistical because different players grab different numbers of rebounds — the data anticipates variability. The other three each have one fixed answer.

optional.stem: Why does "How many minutes does each player spend warming up?" anticipate variability?
choices: Because different players warm up for different amounts of time
Because warming up happens before every game
Because minutes are always a number
Because every player warms up for exactly 10 minutes
optional.explanation: A statistical question anticipates variability. Different players spend different amounts of time warming up, so the collected data will vary from player to player.

optional.label: Each weak question on the left has one fixed answer. Match it to the improved version that anticipates variability.

pairs.term: How many players are on the roster?
pairs.match: How many points does each player on the roster average?

pairs.term: What is the score of the championship game?
pairs.match: How many points does each team score across the season's games?

pairs.term: What time does the game tip off?
pairs.match: What time does each fan arrive at the arena?

pairs.term: How long is a regulation court?
pairs.match: How far from the basket does each player make most of their shots?

optional.title: Level 2 Extension: Catch the Analyst's Mistake

workedExample.label: Question
workedExample.work: How many free throws did each player make during the tournament?

workedExample.label: Analyst says
workedExample.work: This is NOT a statistical question.

workedExample.label: Analyst's reason
workedExample.work: It asks about free throws, and a free throw is always worth 1 point, so the answer never changes.

optional.correctWork: The value of one free throw is fixed, but the question asks how many free throws EACH player MADE during the tournament. Different players make different numbers of free throws, so the data varies from player to player. This IS a statistical question because it anticipates variability.
hints: Look at what the question is actually counting: it is counting how many free throws each player made, not the point value of one free throw.
Ask yourself: will every player make the same number of free throws? If the answers vary, the question is statistical.

practice.commonMistake: A common mistake in Statistical Questions and Data is skipping the key idea: "If you expect the answers to vary, it is a statistical question." — always check your work against this rule before you submit.

connect.scenario: The school newspaper wants to write a story about the girls' soccer team. A reporter suggests asking: "How many goals has the team scored total?" The editor says they should ask: "How many goals did each player score this season?"
connect.promptQuestion: Which question is statistical? Why would the editor's question give the newspaper more interesting data?
connect.prompt: The reporter's question is ___ because ___. The editor's question is ___ because ___. The editor's question is better for a story because ___.

exitTicket.stem:
Which question is a statistical question?
A) How many students are in the school?
B) How many hours does each student in 6th grade exercise per week?
C) What time does school start?
D) How many days are in a week?
choices: B) How many hours does each student exercise per week?
A) How many students are in the school?
C) What time does school start?
D) How many days are in a week?
exitTicket.explanation: "How many hours does each student exercise per week?" is statistical because different students exercise different amounts — the data has variability.

turnAndTalk.question: The coach asks, "How tall is our point guard?" and "How tall is each player on the roster?" Which one is a statistical question, and why?
turnAndTalk.kernel: A statistical question expects answers that vary across many cases.

stems.en: The other question is not statistical because it has only ___ answer.
stems.es: La otra pregunta no es estadística porque tiene solo ___ respuesta.

turnAndTalk.listenFor: Students identify "How tall is each player?" as statistical because heights vary, while "How tall is our point guard?" has one fixed answer.
turnAndTalk.extend: Take a non-statistical question and reword it so it becomes statistical. Explain exactly what you changed to create variability.
extendStems: I changed it to "___" so the answers will ___.
Now it is statistical because the data will ___ from player to player.

turnAndTalk.question: While sorting the questions, what clue tells you instantly that a question will need DATA from many players to answer?
turnAndTalk.kernel: Questions that ask about "each" player usually anticipate variability.

stems.en: I know it needs data because it asks about ___.
stems.es: Sé que necesita datos porque pregunta sobre ___.

stems.en: The answers will be different because ___.

turnAndTalk.listenFor: A strong answer notes words like "each" or "per player" signal variability, so you must collect data from many cases rather than look up one fact.
turnAndTalk.extend: Is it possible for a question with a numerical answer to still be NOT statistical? Justify with an example from the basketball league.
extendStems: Yes, a question like "___" has numbers but is not statistical because ___.
It is not statistical even with numbers since the answer ___.

turnAndTalk.question: The newspaper editor prefers "How many goals did each player score?" over "How many goals did the team score total?" Which gives more interesting data for a story?
turnAndTalk.kernel: A statistical question reveals variability that a single total hides.

stems.en: The editor's question is better because it shows ___ among players.
stems.es: La pregunta del editor es mejor porque muestra ___ entre los jugadores.

stems.en: The total only gives ___ number, so it hides ___.
stems.es: El total solo da ___ número, así que oculta ___.

turnAndTalk.listenFor: Students explain the per-player question is statistical and shows variability (who scored most/least), while the total is one fixed number that hides individual differences.
turnAndTalk.extend: Critique the reporter's plan: when might a single total actually be the RIGHT thing to report instead of per-player data? Defend your view.
extendStems: A total would be better when the reader wants to know ___.
But per-player data is better here because the story is about ___.

turnAndTalk.question: Think of one statistical question you could ask about students in this room. What variability do you expect in the answers?
turnAndTalk.kernel: A good statistical question is answered by collecting varied data, not one fact.
turnAndTalk.listenFor: Students pose a question whose answers vary across classmates and name the variability they expect (e.g., different bedtimes, different shoe sizes).
turnAndTalk.extend: Generalize: what is the one test you can always run on any question to decide if it is statistical? State your rule in one sentence.
extendStems: My rule is: a question is statistical if ___.
I can test any question by asking whether the answers will ___.

turnAndTalk.question: During practice on Statistical Questions and Data, what strategy did you use when a problem felt tricky?
turnAndTalk.kernel: If you expect the answers to vary, it is a statistical question.

stems.en: I know it needs data because it asks about ___.
stems.es: Sé que necesita datos porque pregunta sobre ___.

stems.en: I knew my answer was reasonable because ___.
stems.es: Supe que mi respuesta era razonable porque ___.

turnAndTalk.listenFor: Listen for students naming a specific strategy tied to 6.SP.1 — not just "I multiplied." They should connect steps to the key idea.
turnAndTalk.extend: How would you help a classmate who made the common mistake from today's lesson? Use one vocabulary word.
extendStems: I would tell them to ___ first because ___.
The mistake happens when you ___ instead of ___.

learningTonight.en: Tell statistical questions from non-statistical ones so the team collects data worth analyzing.

conceptSteps.en: You are the new data analyst for the Riverside Hoops youth league.
conceptSteps.es: Lean la historia de la misión juntos antes de empezar los ejercicios.

conceptSteps.en: If you expect the answers to vary, it is a statistical question.
conceptSteps.es: Verifiquen cada paso con la idea clave de la lección.

watchFor.en: Listen for "Statistical Question" — A question where the answers will be different for different people.
watchFor.es: Escucha "Pregunta estadística" — Una pregunta cuyas respuestas serán distintas para distintas personas.

watchFor.en: Listen for "Data" — Facts and numbers you collect, like answers to a survey.
watchFor.es: Escucha "Datos" — Datos y números que recoges, como respuestas a una encuesta.

watchFor.en: Listen for "Variability" — How spread out the numbers are.
watchFor.es: Escucha "Variabilidad" — Qué tan separados están los números.

tryTogether.scenarioEn: You are the new data analyst for the Riverside Hoops youth league. The head coach drops a stack of questions on your desk — some will yield rich data full of variety, others just one fixed fact. Master statistical questions and you decide what the front office actually investigates.
tryTogether.scenarioEs: Usen la historia de la misión para conectar la matemática con un problema real.

steps.en: Pick one practice problem and solve it on paper together.
steps.es: Elijan un problema de práctica y resuélvanlo en papel juntos.
steps.hintEs: Hablen de cada paso en voz alta.

steps.en: Check: does your answer follow — If you expect the answers to vary, it is a statistical question.?
steps.es: Verifiquen: ¿su respuesta sigue — If you expect the answers to vary, it is a statistical question.?
steps.hint: This is the flagship lesson's deepest idea.
steps.hintEs: Esta es la idea más profunda de la lección estrella.

say.en: What part of the mission story helps here?
say.es: ¿Qué parte de la historia de la misión ayuda aquí?

say.en: Let's re-read the key idea together.

dontSay.en: This flagship lesson is too hard — skip it.
dontSay.es: Esta lección estrella es muy difícil — sáltenla.

printables.name: Data Investigation Proposal
printables.desc: Teams design a statistical survey question, predict variability, and collect a small data set.

printables.name: Statistical Questions and Data — Color by Number
printables.desc: Solve each problem, then color by the answer to reveal a picture.

printables.name: Statistical Questions and Data — Vocabulary Word Search
printables.desc: Find this lesson's key vocabulary terms in the puzzle.

printables.name: Statistical Questions and Data — MCAP-Style Practice
printables.desc: MCAP-style selected- and constructed-response practice items.
